title: Finding a Centroid
description: Given a tree with n nodes, find a node that, when chosen as the root, has all of its subtrees of size at most floor(n/2). If multiple nodes satisfy this, output any one of them.

examples.explanation: Rooting at 3 splits the tree into subtrees of sizes 2 and 2 which are <= floor(5/2)=2.

constraints: n - 1 edges; input forms a tree

mcqChain.questionText: If n = 1 (a single node with no edges), which node(s) are valid centroids?

options.label: Any node with an even index

mcqChain.explanation: With one node, choosing it as root gives no subtrees, so all (zero) subtrees have size 0 ≤ floor(1/2)=0. Node 1 is valid.

wrongExplanations.1: A centroid always exists in a non-empty tree.
wrongExplanations.2: Nodes are numbered from 1..n, so 0 is invalid.

mcqChain.questionText: Consider the chain 1-2-3-4-5. Which node is a centroid?
mcqChain.explanation: Root at 3 ⇒ subtrees sizes 2 and 2, both ≤ floor(5/2)=2. Root at 4 ⇒ subtrees sizes 3 and 1 (3>2), so 4 is not valid.

wrongExplanations.2: At 4, the larger subtree has size 3, which violates the limit.
wrongExplanations.0: At 2, the larger subtree has size 3, which violates the limit.

mcqChain.hint: Check the sizes of the largest subtrees when rooting at each candidate.

mcqChain.questionText: For a star with center 1 and leaves 2..5, which node is a centroid?
mcqChain.explanation: Root at 1 ⇒ subtrees (leaves) are size 1 ≤ floor(5/2)=2. A leaf as root has a subtree of size 4, which is too big.

wrongExplanations.1: At a leaf, the subtree containing the center has size 4 (>2).

mcqChain.hint: Compute the size of the largest child subtree for each candidate.

mcqChain.questionText: To check if a node u is a centroid while doing calculations with a fixed root (say root=1), what sizes must be ≤ floor(n/2) for u?

options.label: Only the sizes of u's child subtrees (in the fixed-root tree)
The size of the component above u (if any) only
The maximum among: all child subtree sizes of u and the 'outside' size n - size[u]
The degree of u must be ≤ floor(n/2)

mcqChain.explanation: With a fixed root, u may have a part 'above' it. Largest piece is max(child sizes, n - size[u]). This must be ≤ floor(n/2).

wrongExplanations.0: This ignores the part not in u's subtree relative to the fixed root.
wrongExplanations.1: This ignores u's child subtrees which can be large.

mcqChain.hint: Think about components formed if you remove u from the tree.

mcqChain.questionText: Given n nodes and n-1 edges forming a tree, which statements are always true?

options.label: The graph is connected and acyclic
The graph has exactly one cycle
There is at least one node with degree 0
Edges can be more than n-1 in a tree

mcqChain.explanation: A tree is connected with no cycles and has exactly n-1 edges. There are no isolated nodes if n>1.

wrongExplanations.2: All nodes are connected if n>1; isolated degree 0 nodes don't occur.

mcqChain.hint: Recall basic tree properties.

mcqChain.questionText: A naive strategy: for each node, root the tree at it and count the sizes of all subtrees. What is the rough time if each counting takes O(n)?
mcqChain.explanation: Doing O(n) work for each of n nodes is O(n^2), which is too slow for n up to 2e5.

wrongExplanations.0: That's only if counting is done once.
wrongExplanations.1: Nothing suggests a log factor here.

mcqChain.hint: Multiply the per-node cost by the number of nodes.

mcqChain.questionText: To decide if a node u is a centroid using one pass of size computations, what key information do we need for every node u?

options.label: The sizes of all neighbor-side components: each child's subtree size and n - size[u]
The distance from u to all other nodes
The number of leaves in u's subtree

mcqChain.explanation: We need the sizes of all pieces after removing u: child subtree sizes and the outside part n - size[u].

wrongExplanations.0: Degree alone doesn't give component sizes.
wrongExplanations.2: Distances are not directly related to subtree sizes here.

mcqChain.hint: Think in terms of component sizes when u is removed.

mcqChain.questionText: While recursively visiting neighbors to compute subtree sizes, how do we prevent infinite back-and-forth along an edge?
mcqChain.diagram:
u -- v
visit u -> v, then avoid going back to u from v

options.label: Ignore neighbors with a smaller index
Track the parent node and skip it when visiting neighbors
Sort neighbors and visit in order

mcqChain.explanation: Passing the parent and skipping it avoids revisiting the caller through the same edge.

wrongExplanations.0: Index-based filtering doesn't reflect tree structure.
wrongExplanations.2: You must visit all children, not just one.

mcqChain.hint: Carry the previous node in the recursive call and skip it.

mcqChain.questionText: We will use an adjacency list for the tree. For nodes labeled 1..n, which structure is suitable?
mcqChain.explanation: A list of lists sized n+1 supports 1-based indexing and storing neighbors.

wrongExplanations.1: Sets could work but range(n) would be 0..n-1, mismatching 1..n indexing.
wrongExplanations.2: An empty dict alone doesn't define neighbors per node.

mcqChain.hint: We want 1-based indexing and a container of neighbors for each node.

mcqChain.questionText: How do we define the subtree size size[u] using a recursive visit that skips the parent p?

options.label: size[u] = sum(size[v] for v in adj[u])
size[u] = 1 + sum(size[v] for v in children of u excluding p)

mcqChain.explanation: Each node counts itself plus the sizes of its child subtrees (excluding the parent side).

wrongExplanations.0: This double-counts and includes the parent side.
wrongExplanations.2: Degree is unrelated to subtree size except in special cases.

mcqChain.hint: Include the node itself and add sizes from recursive children only.

mcqChain.questionText: Suppose for node u we have child subtree sizes [2, 3, 1] and n = 10. If size[u] = 1 + 2 + 3 + 1, what is the largest piece when considering u with a fixed-root perspective?
mcqChain.explanation: size[u]=7 ⇒ outside = n - size[u] = 3. Max among child sizes (2,3,1) and outside (3) is 3.

wrongExplanations.1: 6 doesn't appear among child sizes or outside; outside is 3.

mcqChain.hint: Compute outside = n - size[u] and take the max with child sizes.

mcqChain.questionText: If u is the root of our traversal (e.g., node 1 initially), what is the value of the outside part n - size[u]?
mcqChain.explanation: The root's subtree includes all nodes, so size[root]=n and n - size[root] = 0.

wrongExplanations.0: n - n is 0 at the root.
wrongExplanations.1: This is the subtree size, not the outside part.

mcqChain.hint: How many nodes are not in the root’s subtree?

mcqChain.questionText: For n = 1 with size[1] = 1, what is the largest piece for node 1 when computing max(child sizes, n - size[1])?
mcqChain.explanation: There are no children, so child max is 0. Outside is n - size[1] = 0. The largest piece is 0.

wrongExplanations.0: No child subtree has size 1; outside is also 0.

mcqChain.hint: Empty set max can be treated as 0 here.

mcqChain.questionText: If multiple nodes tie for the smallest largest-piece size, what should we output?
mcqChain.explanation: The problem allows returning any centroid when multiple exist.

wrongExplanations.0: No tie-breaking rule is required by the problem.

mcqChain.hint: Check the Output specification.

mcqChain.questionText: Set up fast input and allow deep recursion. Choose the best snippet.
mcqChain.explanation: We need sys and to raise recursion limit for deep trees.

wrongExplanations.1: math has no setrecursionlimit; this would error.
wrongExplanations.3: setcheckinterval is unrelated to recursion depth.

mcqChain.hint: Use the standard module that controls recursion depth.

mcqChain.questionText: Read n from stdin efficiently.
mcqChain.explanation: Reading a single line and converting to int is correct and efficient.

wrongExplanations.1: read() reads the entire file; also missing int conversion.
wrongExplanations.2: split() returns a list; int() on a list will fail.

mcqChain.hint: Use readline and convert to int.

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize the adjacency list for nodes 1..n.
mcqChain.explanation: We need n+1 slots for 1-based indexing; lists of lists are fine for neighbors.

wrongExplanations.1: Size n would make index n invalid.
wrongExplanations.3: An empty dict alone doesn't store per-node lists.

mcqChain.hint: Account for 1-based node ids.

mcqChain.questionText: Read n-1 edges and fill the adjacency list appropriately.

options.label:
for _ in range(n-1):
    a, b = map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split())
    adj[a].append(b)
    adj[b].append(a)

for _ in range(n):
    a, b = map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split())
    adj[a].append(b)
    adj[b].append(a)

mcqChain.explanation: Each undirected edge must be added both ways; there are exactly n-1 edges.

wrongExplanations.1: Missing the reverse addition; graph would be one-directional.
wrongExplanations.2: There are n-1 edges, not n.

mcqChain.hint: Tree edges are undirected.
mcqChain.codeSnippet:
for _ in range(n-1):
    a, b = map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split())
    adj[a].append(b)
    adj[b].append(a)

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize storage for subtree sizes and tracking the current best centroid candidate.
mcqChain.explanation: Use 1-based size array; initialize best_max to n and best_node to any valid index like 1.

wrongExplanations.1: Off-by-one in size array and invalid initial best values.
wrongExplanations.3: Variable names don't match later usage and tuple complicates updates.

mcqChain.hint: Start with a safe worst-case best_max.

mcqChain.questionText: Write the function header and the part that accumulates subtree sizes while skipping the parent.
mcqChain.explanation: We must skip the parent, recurse to children, and add their sizes; declare globals to update best later.

wrongExplanations.2: Missing parent check risks infinite recursion.
wrongExplanations.3: Adding 1 per neighbor counts edges, not subtree sizes.

mcqChain.hint: Initialize with 1 and recursively add sizes from valid neighbors.

mcqChain.questionText: Inside visit, compute the largest piece for u and update the best candidate if improved.
mcqChain.explanation: Largest piece is the max of child sizes and the outside part. Update best when this value decreases.

wrongExplanations.1: Using min on child sizes and comparing inversely is incorrect logic.
wrongExplanations.3: Summing child sizes is the total below u, not the largest piece.

mcqChain.hint: We care about the maximum component size after removing u.
mcqChain.codeLabel: Compute max piece and update best

mcqChain.questionText: Call the traversal and print the result.
mcqChain.explanation: Start at any valid node like 1 with parent 0, then print the best_node tracked globally.

wrongExplanations.1: Node 0 doesn't exist; parent can be 0 though, but starting node must be in 1..n.
wrongExplanations.3: visit returns None; it updates globals instead.

mcqChain.hint: The function updates global best_node; it doesn't return it.

mcqChain.questionText:
Run the code conceptually on the sample:
5
1 2
2 3
3 4
3 5
Which node will be printed?
mcqChain.explanation: At node 3, the largest piece is 2 (subtrees of 2 and 2). Others have a largest piece of 3. So 3 is printed.

wrongExplanations.0: At 2, the piece through 3 has size 3.
wrongExplanations.2: At 4, the piece through 3 has size 4.

mcqChain.hint: Compute size[u] and then max(child sizes, n - size[u]) for each u.

mcqChain.questionText: Consider a star with center 1 and leaves 2..4 (n=4). What will the program output?
mcqChain.explanation: At 1, largest piece is 1. At any leaf, the piece through 1 is size 3 (> floor(4/2)=2).

wrongExplanations.1: Leafs have a large opposite piece through the center.

mcqChain.hint: Check largest piece at center vs at leaf.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity of the solution?
mcqChain.explanation: Each edge is processed a constant number of times during one traversal; overall linear time.

wrongExplanations.1: There is no sorting or log factor.
wrongExplanations.2: We do not recompute sizes per node.

mcqChain.hint: Count how many times each edge and node are visited.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the additional space used by the algorithm (excluding input storage for the adjacency list)?

options.label: O(n) for size array and recursion stack

mcqChain.explanation: We use size[1..n] and recursion stack up to tree height, both O(n) in worst case.

wrongExplanations.1: The size array alone is O(n).

mcqChain.hint: Consider the arrays and the maximum recursion depth on a path.

mcqChain.questionText: Why do we set a high recursion limit at the start?

options.label: Trees can be very deep (e.g., a chain), causing many nested calls

mcqChain.explanation: A path-like tree of length n could require n nested calls; default limit (~1000) is too low.

wrongExplanations.1: Recursion limit does not affect I/O speed.
wrongExplanations.2: Raising recursion limit doesn't reduce memory usage.

mcqChain.hint: Consider the worst-case shape of the tree.

finalCodeSnippets:
for _ in range(n-1):
    a, b = map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split())
    adj[a].append(b)
    adj[b].append(a)

reasoningChain.question: What exactly defines a centroid and how does it relate to subtree sizes and the overall tree size?
reasoningChain.insight: A centroid is a node where the largest piece after considering all neighbor-side components (child subtrees and the outside part) is ≤ floor(n/2).

reasoningChain.question: How can we check this condition efficiently for all nodes without re-rooting the entire tree repeatedly?
reasoningChain.insight: Compute subtree sizes once using a recursive visit while skipping the parent, then for each node compute its largest piece as max(child sizes, n - size[u]).

reasoningChain.question: Why must we include the 'outside' part n - size[u] when evaluating a node with a fixed initial root?
reasoningChain.insight: Because from a fixed root's perspective, the component above u forms an additional piece that must be considered in the centroid condition.

reasoningChain.question: What data structures and traversal order allow us to compute sizes and evaluate all nodes in linear time?
reasoningChain.insight: An adjacency list for the tree and a single recursive pass visiting neighbors (skipping the parent) lets us compute size[u] for all u and evaluate each node’s largest piece.

reasoningChain.question: How do we track and update the best centroid candidate during the traversal?
reasoningChain.insight: Maintain best_max (current minimal largest piece) and best_node; for each u, compute max_part and update if max_part < best_max.

reasoningChain.question: What are the time and space implications of this approach for n up to 2e5?
reasoningChain.insight: Time is O(n) since each edge is processed a constant number of times; extra space is O(n) for sizes and the recursion stack in the worst case.
